chapter_title: ANALYTICAL REASONING - Evaluating Given Courses of Action

solutions.solution:
Statement: A group of school students was reported to be enjoying at a water pool during school hours.
Courses of action: I. The Principal should contact the parents of the students and inform them about the incident with a warning for the future.
II. Such disciplinary action must be taken against the students and all other students should be made aware of it.
Explanation: Both the courses of action are necessary as parents must be informed and disciplinary action should be taken.

Statement: India spends a lot of money every year. Not even a single medal could be bagged by the players in the last several international sporting events forcing the players to participate in the Olympic Games.
Courses of action: I. India should stop sending players to the Olympic Games.
II. The Government should immediately set up an enquiry commission to find out the reason for the dismal performance.
Explanation: Course I is not a solution. Course II is appropriate as enquiry will help find the actual problems.

Statement: Every year, at the beginning or at the end of the monsoons, there some cases of conjunctivitis, but this year it seems to be a major epidemic witnessed after nearly four years.
Courses of action: I. Precautionary measures should be taken after every four years to check this epidemic.
II. People should be advised to drink boiled water during the water season.
Explanation: Course I talks about measures after four years which is not immediate. Course II provides immediate solution by advising boiled water.

Statement: Footpaths of a busy road are overcrowded with vendors selling their items.
Courses of action: I. The help of police should be sought to drive them away.
II. Some space should be provided to them where they can earn their bread without blocking footpaths.
Explanation: Course I is temporary solution. Course II provides permanent solution by providing alternative space.

Statement: Since its launching in 1981, Vayudoot has so far accumulated losses amounting to Rs. 153 crore during the last ten years.
Courses of action: I. Vayudoot should be directed to reduce wasteful expenditure and to increase passenger fare.
II. An amount of about Rs. 300 crore should be provided by the Government to make the airline economically viable.
Explanation: Both courses are necessary - reducing costs and government support to make it viable.

Statement: The Indian electronic component industry is venturing into the West European markets faces tough competition from the Japanese.
Courses of action: I. India should search for other international markets for its products.
II. India should improve the quality of the electronic components to compete with the Japanese in important markets.
Explanation: Both courses are complementary - searching new markets and improving quality to compete better.

Statement: Recent study shows that children below five die in the cities of the developing countries mainly of diarrhoea and parasitic intestinal worms.
Courses of action: I. Governments of the developing countries should take adequate measures to improve the hygienic condition of the cities.
II. Children below five years in the cities of the developing countries need to be brought under constant medication.
Explanation: Course I addresses root cause by improving hygiene. Course II is reactive approach through medication.

Statement: The sale of a particular product has gone down considerably, causing great concern to the company.
Courses of action: I. The company should make a proper study of rival products in the market.
II. The price of the product should be reduced and the quality improved.
Explanation: Both courses are necessary - market study and product improvement through price reduction and quality enhancement.

Statement: The police department has come under a cloud with recent revelations that at least two senior police officials are suspected to have been involved in illegal sale of a large quantity of weapons from the state police armoury.
Courses of action: I. A thorough investigation should be ordered by the State Government to bring out all those who are involved in the illegal sale of arms.
II. State police armoury should be kept under the Central Government's control.
Explanation: Course I is immediate need for investigation. Course II provides long-term security measure.

Statement: One of the problems the food-processing industry is facing is the irregular supply of raw material. The producers of raw material are not getting a reasonable price.
Courses of action: I. The government should regulate the supply of raw material to other industries also.
II. The government should announce an attractive package to ensure regular supply of raw material for the food-processing industry.
Explanation: Course I is general regulation. Course II specifically addresses the food-processing industry's needs.

Statement: A leading US multinational engineering and construction firm is keen to invest in India in a variety of sectors ranging from power to land management.
Courses of action: I. Such multinational companies should not be allowed to operate in India.
II. India should encourage multinational companies from other developed countries to invest in power sectors to bring in a competitive climate.
Explanation: Course I is negative approach. Course II encourages healthy competition through foreign investment.

Statement: Mr X, an active member of the union, often insults his superiors in the office with his rude behaviour.
Explanation: This appears to be an incomplete solution as the courses of action are not visible in this excerpt.
